steps.dialogue: You just solved a problem by comparing both [event:699616403fc91162] fractions to the halfway point. When we use a line like the one at [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]one half[/fraction] to help compare fractions, that line is called [event:6994d24da2040c3c]a [vocab]benchmark[/vocab].

A benchmark is like a signpost or a marker, something you can measure things against. You used [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]one half[/fraction] as your benchmark to [event:6994d34a87954cd8] figure out that [fraction numerator=3 denominator=4]three fourths[/fraction] is [event:6994d358cd6d8959] greater than [fraction numerator=2 denominator=6]two sixths[/fraction].
We have used [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]one half[/fraction] as a benchmark with bars, now let's use a [event:699616fe4bf81f87] benchmark on a number line.

These lines show the same fractions as the bars earlier, [event:699390e6806da0d7][fraction numerator=3 denominator=4]three fourths[/fraction] and [event:69939110bd3ca045][fraction numerator=2 denominator=6]two sixths[/fraction], just in a different form. The benchmark at [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]one half[/fraction] works the same as before. Look at where each fraction sits on its number line. Which fraction is greater than [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]one half[/fraction]?

prompt.text: Which fraction is greater than [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]one half[/fraction]?

step.dialogue: Look again at where each fraction is positioned. compare it to the [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]one half[/fraction] benchmark line.

metadata.description: Highlight the benchmark line at 1/2, then highlight 3/4 showing it's to the right, and 2/6 showing it's to the left

step.dialogue: Remember from the warmup, [fraction numerator=3 denominator=4]three fourths[/fraction] was [event:69939179dec5c578]above halfway and [fraction numerator=2 denominator=6]two sixths[/fraction] was [event:699391b5757b75d0]below. The number line shows the same thing, just in a different way.

metadata.description: Animate pointing to 3/4 on top line showing it's right of benchmark, then point to 2/6 on bottom line showing it's left of benchmark

step.dialogue: We'll work it out together. On the top number line, [fraction numerator=3 denominator=4]three fourths[/fraction] sits to [event:699394850e3399e8]the right of the [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]one half[/fraction] benchmark that means it's greater than half. On the bottom line, [fraction numerator=2 denominator=6]two sixths[/fraction] sits to [event:699394ab4cc6ee76]the left of [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]one half[/fraction], that means it's less than half. [event:699394c3171c0d7f]Same as what we saw with bars.

on_correct.dialogue: Exactly! On the number line, you can see [fraction numerator=3 denominator=4]three fourths[/fraction] is past the [event:69939d47f7a3cc70]benchmark at [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]one half[/fraction], while [fraction numerator=2 denominator=6]two sixths[/fraction] [event:69939e277b32630e] is before it.

steps.dialogue: Benchmarks [event:699618a3f6ec605c]help you compare fractions no matter how they're shown to us visually. [event:69966fd8d04872fe]With bars or [event:6996701436a71b6c]number lines.
Both [fraction numerator=3 denominator=4]three fourths[/fraction] and [fraction numerator=2 denominator=3]two thirds[/fraction] are above the benchmark. Which fraction is greater?

step.dialogue: Let's check again. Both points are above half, but which one is further from the benchmark line?

metadata.description: Show visual arrows from the benchmark line to each fraction, highlighting that 3/4 has a longer arrow
Animate selecting 3/4 as the answer, show comparison arrows from benchmark to both fractions

step.dialogue: Let me show you. [fraction numerator=3 denominator=4]Three fourths[/fraction] is [event:6993a865fffba5a1]farther from the benchmark than [event:6993a87aa06cd9c5][fraction numerator=2 denominator=3]two thirds[/fraction]. When both fractions are above [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]one half[/fraction], the one that's farther [event:6993a9942d0c9759]away is greater. So [event:6993aba2ac49a649][fraction numerator=3 denominator=4]three fourths[/fraction] is greater than [fraction numerator=2 denominator=3]two thirds[/fraction].

on_correct.dialogue: Yes. You noticed how far each fraction is from [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]one half[/fraction]. [event:6994c8aba9a123d3][fraction numerator=2 denominator=3]Two thirds[/fraction] is closer to [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]one half[/fraction]. [event:6994c8d42c1af437][fraction numerator=3 denominator=4]Three fourths[/fraction] is further away, which means it's larger. So [event:6996713d2044dd69][fraction numerator=3 denominator=4]three fourths[/fraction] is greater than [fraction numerator=2 denominator=3]two thirds[/fraction].

steps.dialogue: Let's test this benchmark method again to reinforce the idea. Compare [fraction numerator=3 denominator=8]three eighths[/fraction] and [fraction numerator=5 denominator=8]five eighths[/fraction].

prompt.text: Compare [fraction numerator=3 denominator=8]three eighths[/fraction] and [fraction numerator=5 denominator=8]five eighths[/fraction]

step.dialogue: Not quite. Try checking each fraction against [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]one half[/fraction] first.

metadata.description: Highlight the 1/2 benchmark on both number lines and show where 3/8 and 5/8 fall relative to it
Animate selecting 5/8 as the answer, highlight 4/8 as the benchmark equivalent

step.dialogue: Follow along with me: [fraction numerator=4 denominator=8]four eighths[/fraction] [event:69964f415905e3ad]equals [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]one half[/fraction]. Since [fraction numerator=3 denominator=8]three eighths[/fraction] is [event:69964f7fac330183]less than [fraction numerator=4 denominator=8]four eighths[/fraction] and [fraction numerator=5 denominator=8]five eighths[/fraction] is [event:69964fb1e45d7267]greater than [fraction numerator=4 denominator=8]four eighths[/fraction], we know [fraction numerator=3 denominator=8]three eighths[/fraction] is [event:6996509b9c089b83]less than [fraction numerator=5 denominator=8]five eighths[/fraction].

on_correct.dialogue: That's right. [fraction numerator=3 denominator=8]Three eighths[/fraction] is [event:6996516fe38d8ef1]left of [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]one half[/fraction], and [fraction numerator=5 denominator=8]five eighths[/fraction] is to [event:69965188c97a1b03]the right of [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]one half[/fraction], so [fraction numerator=3 denominator=8]three eighths[/fraction] is [event:699651bd04e2f279]less than [fraction numerator=5 denominator=8]five eighths[/fraction]. The benchmark method confirms what you could also figure out with the matching denominators method.

steps.dialogue: You're figuring out new strategies to compare fractions! You've used [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]one half[/fraction] as a benchmark. [event:699742521838e92b]Now we can ask: what happens when both fractions are [event:699743e7e3584623] close to 1?
Here, both fractions are close to 1, so [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]one half[/fraction] won't help as much. We can [event:6994de962d182c8d]use 1 as the benchmark instead.
Look at what's missing from each fraction to make a whole number. Which fraction has the smaller missing piece?

prompt.text: Select the fraction with the smaller missing piece.

step.dialogue: Think about it once more. Which missing piece is smaller, [fraction numerator=1 denominator=8]one eighth[/fraction] or [fraction numerator=1 denominator=6]one sixth[/fraction]? Remember: more pieces means smaller pieces.

metadata.description: Highlight the missing pieces on both bars, visually show that 1/8 is smaller than 1/6
Animate selecting 1/8, highlight both missing pieces side by side to compare sizes

step.dialogue: Let me show you: When we divide into 8 [event:6996554da5e7839a]pieces, each piece is smaller [event:69965566bfe47470]than when we divide into 6. So [event:699656346218e906][fraction numerator=1 denominator=8]one eighth[/fraction] is less than [fraction numerator=1 denominator=6]one sixth[/fraction]. That means [event:69965595ef26de02][fraction numerator=7 denominator=8]seven eighths[/fraction] has less missing.

on_correct.dialogue: Right. [event:699656b2069d4b57][fraction numerator=1 denominator=8]One eighth[/fraction] is smaller than [fraction numerator=1 denominator=6]one sixth[/fraction].

steps.dialogue: Now let's compare the fractions. If [fraction numerator=7 denominator=8]seven eighths[/fraction] needs only [fraction numerator=1 denominator=8]one eighth[/fraction] to reach 1, and [fraction numerator=5 denominator=6]five sixths[/fraction] needs [fraction numerator=1 denominator=6]one sixth[/fraction] to reach 1, which fraction is greater?

prompt.text: Compare [fraction numerator=7 denominator=8]seven eighths[/fraction] and [fraction numerator=5 denominator=6]five sixths[/fraction]

step.dialogue: Think about it again. You figured out [fraction numerator=1 denominator=8]one eighth[/fraction] is smaller than [fraction numerator=1 denominator=6]one sixth[/fraction]. What does that tell you about which fraction is closer to 1?

metadata.description: Highlight the distance from each fraction to 1, showing 7/8 is closer
Animate selecting 7/8, show both fractions with their distances to 1 clearly marked

step.dialogue: Here's how we can work this out: [fraction numerator=7 denominator=8]Seven eighths[/fraction] needs [event:6996586ccf39a46c][fraction numerator=1 denominator=8]one eighth[/fraction] more to reach 1. [fraction numerator=5 denominator=6]Five sixths[/fraction] needs [event:6996594b0eb87181][fraction numerator=1 denominator=6]one sixth[/fraction] more. Since [event:69965a1f000559d7][fraction numerator=1 denominator=8]one eighth[/fraction] is [event:69965b7a73b711e4]smaller than [fraction numerator=1 denominator=6]one sixth[/fraction], that means [fraction numerator=7 denominator=8]seven eighths[/fraction] has less distance to travel. Less distance means closer to 1, which means [fraction numerator=7 denominator=8]seven eighths[/fraction] is the greater fraction.

on_correct.dialogue: Yes! [fraction numerator=7 denominator=8]Seven eighths[/fraction] needs [event:699661930b176344][fraction numerator=1 denominator=8]one eighth[/fraction] to reach 1, while [fraction numerator=5 denominator=6]five sixths[/fraction] needs [event:699661a6e2ba40aa][fraction numerator=1 denominator=6]one sixth[/fraction]. Since eighths are smaller pieces than sixths, [fraction numerator=7 denominator=8]seven eighths[/fraction] is [event:699661c6f9682bd2]greater than [fraction numerator=5 denominator=6]five sixths[/fraction].

steps.dialogue: You've been comparing fractions using benchmarks with number lines and bars. Now I'll show you how to write that reasoning with mathematical symbols [event:69967205cf72d181]without the help of number lines or bars.

I'll show you how I compare the fractions [event:69967282ef68395e][fraction numerator=7 denominator=10]seven tenths[/fraction] and [fraction numerator=2 denominator=8]two eighths[/fraction] using a benchmark of [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]one half[/fraction]. Let's walk through how I write the benchmark reasoning symbolically.

First, I compare [fraction numerator=7 denominator=10]seven tenths[/fraction] to the benchmark of [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]one half[/fraction][event:69967b4f473c6ac8]. Half of 10 is 5, and I have [event:6995040f0fad7841]7 parts. That's [event:69967cbea7bd14b7]more [event:699504487dd777a7]than half. [event:69967cdd84edfc70]So I write: [event:69967c5e39100413][fraction numerator=7 denominator=10]seven tenths[/fraction] is [event:699503b73e30cd0d]greater than [event:699503f2b383b216][fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]one half[/fraction]. The symbol opens toward the bigger fraction.

Next, I compare [fraction numerator=2 denominator=8]two eighths[/fraction] to the benchmark of [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]one half[/fraction][event:69967d8d6b752b82]. Half of 8 [event:6995073ebbd63f97]is 4, and I only have [event:69950772026a8d9d]2 parts. That's [event:69967dfaac835362]less than [event:699507c6de2db7ff]half. [event:69967e17cf7960ee]So I write: [event:69967e58cca626ca][fraction numerator=2 denominator=8]two eighths[/fraction] is [event:699508df357d0b1a]less than [event:699508ef1e5fa02e][fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]one half[/fraction]. The arrow points to the smaller fraction.

Now I can use those two statements to draw my conclusion. Since [fraction numerator=7 denominator=10]seven tenths[/fraction] is [event:69952d6661a59df9]greater than the benchmark and [fraction numerator=2 denominator=8]two eighths[/fraction] is [event:69952d89c3524ab0]less than the benchmark, [event:69952f14a08dfbc9][fraction numerator=7 denominator=10]seven tenths[/fraction] must be greater than [fraction numerator=2 denominator=8]two eighths[/fraction].

This is called [vocab]justifying symbolically[/vocab], which means you are using the math itself, the numbers and symbols, to show your thinking. This is showing benchmark thinking using mathematical symbols instead of bars or number lines.

The symbols and the visual show the same reasoning. Both prove that [event:6996749c91b9bd63][fraction numerator=7 denominator=10]seven tenths[/fraction] is [event:69967554cf565f72]greater than [event:699675362d7a83e7][fraction numerator=2 denominator=8]two eighths[/fraction].
Now let's review. Which symbol did I use to compare [fraction numerator=2 denominator=8]two eighths[/fraction] to the benchmark?

prompt.text: What symbol shows [fraction numerator=2 denominator=8]two eighths[/fraction] compared to [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]one half[/fraction]?

step.dialogue: Remember, [fraction numerator=2 denominator=8]two eighths[/fraction] is below the benchmark. Which symbol means less than?

metadata.description: Highlight the less than symbol and show 2/8 positioned below 1/2 on a number line
Animate selecting the < symbol, highlight it in the symbolic statement

step.dialogue: Let me show you. We know that [event:69961115fd88e295][fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]one half[/fraction] is the same as [fraction numerator=4 denominator=8]four eighths[/fraction]. Now that they have the same denominator, [event:699614904a8891ab]we can count the pieces. [fraction numerator=2 denominator=8]two eighths[/fraction] is less than [fraction numerator=4 denominator=8]four eighths[/fraction], so [fraction numerator=2 denominator=8]two eighths[/fraction] is [event:699615992ff72eab]less than [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]one half[/fraction]. The less than symbol points to the smaller fraction.

on_correct.dialogue: Exactly. Now you're ready to try writing symbolic justification yourself.

steps.dialogue: You just watched me justify symbolically. Let's review the steps clearly so you can use them. [event:6996172330cec386]Step 1: [event:6996174dd27e0987]Compare the first fraction to [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]one half[/fraction]. Step 2: [event:699617666e1ba6d7]Compare the second fraction to [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]one half[/fraction]. Step 3: [event:6996177835aca97a]Use those comparisons to draw your conclusion.

Now you'll try [vocab]justifying symbolically[/vocab] with new fractions, [event:699618e037c516d6][fraction numerator=4 denominator=6]four sixths[/fraction] and [fraction numerator=2 denominator=5]two fifths[/fraction]. First, [event:699618f9355cf79f]let's compare [fraction numerator=4 denominator=6]four sixths[/fraction] to the benchmark of [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]one half[/fraction].

prompt.text: Compare [fraction numerator=4 denominator=6]four sixths[/fraction] and [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]one half[/fraction].

step.dialogue: [fraction numerator=4 denominator=6]Four sixths[/fraction] means 4 out of 6 parts. Half would be 3 out of 6 parts. Is 4 more than 3?

metadata.description: Show a bar divided into 6 parts with 4 shaded, highlight that 3 would be halfway
Animate selecting the > symbol, show the calculation visually

step.dialogue: I can help you think through this. Half of 6 parts [event:69961b97312e8085]would be 3 parts. Since we have 4 parts, that's [event:69961bba38f2e754]more than half. So [fraction numerator=4 denominator=6]four sixths[/fraction] is [event:69961bc309b39d37]greater than [event:69961bfc75ead6c9][fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]one half[/fraction].

on_correct.dialogue: [event:69961ac8dee6a731]Right. [fraction numerator=4 denominator=6]Four sixths[/fraction] is [event:69961ae6806a6ba0]greater than [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]one half[/fraction].

steps.dialogue: Next, can you compare [fraction numerator=2 denominator=5]two fifths[/fraction] to the benchmark of [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]one half[/fraction]?

prompt.text: Compare [fraction numerator=2 denominator=5]two fifths[/fraction] and [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]one half[/fraction].

step.dialogue: Almost. Think about half of 5 parts. Would that be more or less than 2 parts?

metadata.description: Show a bar divided into 5 parts with 2 shaded, highlight where 2.5 would be
Animate selecting the < symbol, show the calculation visually

step.dialogue: Watch me, and I'll walk [event:69961eb49665c3cb]through this. [event:69961ec0b02a0bc5]Half of 5 parts would be [event:69961ed5e36170f9]here, past [fraction numerator=2 denominator=5]two fifths[/fraction]. Since we only have [event:69961ee95ed323de]2 whole parts, that's less than half. [event:69961f02d0a5c430]So [fraction numerator=2 denominator=5]two fifths[/fraction] is less than [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]one half[/fraction].

on_correct.dialogue: [event:69961c7e89ffee60]Exactly. [fraction numerator=2 denominator=5]Two fifths[/fraction] is [event:69961c8cb8cba15c]less than [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]one half[/fraction].

steps.dialogue: Now, can you use those two comparisons to [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]one half[/fraction] to figure out which is greater: [fraction numerator=4 denominator=6]four sixths[/fraction] or [fraction numerator=2 denominator=5]two fifths[/fraction]?

prompt.text: Compare [fraction numerator=4 denominator=6]four sixths[/fraction] and [fraction numerator=2 denominator=5]two fifths[/fraction]

step.dialogue: Not quite. Think about what you just figured out. Which fraction is above the benchmark? Which is below?

metadata.description: Show both symbolic comparisons to 1/2, then highlight the conclusion

step.dialogue: Think about it [event:6996231c2ba4bc7f]this way: [fraction numerator=4 denominator=6]Four sixths[/fraction] is greater than [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]one half[/fraction]. [event:6996234df7f3c5b7][fraction numerator=2 denominator=5]Two fifths[/fraction] is less than [fraction numerator=1 denominator=2]one half[/fraction]. So which fraction must be greater?

metadata.description: Animate selecting the > symbol, show complete symbolic justification

step.dialogue: Here's how we use the [event:699623eaf567cc50]benchmark. Since [event:699623fe86c41f79][fraction numerator=4 denominator=6]four sixths[/fraction] is above the benchmark and [event:6996240a9af73d06][fraction numerator=2 denominator=5]two fifths[/fraction] is below the benchmark, [event:6996241d0428ad1f][fraction numerator=4 denominator=6]four sixths[/fraction] must be the greater fraction. We write: [event:69962466e08236e6][fraction numerator=4 denominator=6]four sixths[/fraction] is greater than [fraction numerator=2 denominator=5]two fifths[/fraction].

on_correct.dialogue: [event:6997452b3ef5cabf]You got it! You're showing your benchmark reasoning with mathematical symbols. Again, this is called [vocab]justifying symbolically[/vocab].

steps.dialogue: The number line shows exactly what your symbols proved. [fraction numerator=4 denominator=6]Four sixths[/fraction] is to [event:699625bc75344912]the right of the benchmark, [fraction numerator=2 denominator=5]two fifths[/fraction] is to [event:699625dbbf55a081]the left of the benchmark. Your symbolic reasoning matches the visual.
